// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 4 — CLAUDE CODE AS AGENTIC ENGINEERING

Claude Code is a command-line AI agent that operates inside a development environment. This is the first thing to be cle

Chapter 4 — Claude Code as Agentic Engin — Concretely, it can read files across a repository, edit multiple files in a single task, run shell commands — tests, bui
NARRATION

Claude Code is a command-line AI agent that operates inside a development environment. This is the first thing to be clear about, because the word "AI" in "AI coding tool" spans an enormous range. At one end is an autocomplete that finishes a line of code based on the few lines above it. At the other end is an agent that reads a codebase, forms a plan, edits multiple files, runs tests, observes the result, revises its approach, and reports. Claude Code is at the second end.

// SLIDE 02 — THE ACTION SURFACE FOR ENGINEE

THE ACTION SURFACE FOR ENGINEERING WORK.

Read files — Privacy and secrets exposure
Edit files — Behavioral change in a working system
Run tests — Low–moderate
Install packages — Supply-chain risk
NARRATION

Chapter 1 introduced the see/decide/do framework as a way to characterize what an AI system is actually capable of. For a coding agent, that framework produces a more specific picture than it does for a general assistant. What can it see? Every file in the repository scope you define, the output of any command it runs, and any context you provide in the task description. If secrets, API keys, or database passwords live in files within that scope, the agent can see them.

// SLIDE 03 — SEVEN STEPS FROM ISSUE TO DECI

SEVEN STEPS FROM ISSUE TO DECISION.

Define the issue as acceptance criteria.→Bound the permissions before the agent starts.→Review the plan before any action executes.→Authorize bounded edits and commands.→Run verification.
NARRATION

The most useful way to understand agentic engineering supervision is to follow a bounded task from problem statement to merge decision. Each step is a specific human action, not a formality. Define the issue as acceptance criteria. The human writes what is broken, what the correct behavior should be, and how to confirm it. Vague scope , "fix the login bug" , is not enough.

// SLIDE 04 — TWO CATEGORIES THAT REQUIRE SP

TWO CATEGORIES THAT REQUIRE SPECIAL HANDLING.

Two areas of the action surface warrant extra attention because their failure modes are not just bugs but structural vul

Two Categories That Require Special Hand — Secrets and credentials. Coding agents read files. If API keys, database passwords, private keys, or authentication toke
NARRATION

Two areas of the action surface warrant extra attention because their failure modes are not just bugs but structural vulnerabilities. Secrets and credentials. Coding agents read files. If API keys, database passwords, private keys, or authentication tokens live in files within the repository scope, those secrets are inside the agent's observation surface. The safe posture: exclude secrets directories and credential files explicitly from the scope, use a .gitignore-style exclusion or folder separation, and never include credential handling as part of an agentic task.

// SLIDE 05 — THE PROPERTY THAT MAKES ENGINE

THE PROPERTY THAT MAKES ENGINEERING TRACTABLE.

Knowledge work is hard to verify. A document might look correct and contain a subtle error. A summary might omit somethi

The Property That Makes Engineering Trac — Code is different. Code has an oracle. A test either passes or fails. A build either compiles or errors. A linter either
NARRATION

Knowledge work is hard to verify. A document might look correct and contain a subtle error. A summary might omit something important without any visible sign that it is incomplete. Chapter 5 will address exactly this problem. Code is different. Code has an oracle. A test either passes or fails. A build either compiles or errors. A linter either finds a violation or it does not. This is why agentic engineering, despite the elevated action surface, is in some ways the most tractable application of agentic AI.

// SLIDE 06 — THE HUMAN ROLE, RESTATED

THE HUMAN ROLE, RESTATED.

What is the human actually doing in an agentic engineering workflow? Not pressing a button and waiting for the pull requ

The Human Role, Restated — Write the issue clearly enough that the acceptance criteria are testable. Set the permission boundary before the agent s
NARRATION

What is the human actually doing in an agentic engineering workflow? Not pressing a button and waiting for the pull request. The role is: Write the issue clearly enough that the acceptance criteria are testable. Set the permission boundary before the agent starts. Read the plan before authorizing action. Verify with tests and diff, not with the agent's description of success. Make the final accept or reject call.

// SLIDE 07 — WHAT COMPILING DOES NOT PROVE

WHAT COMPILING DOES NOT PROVE.

A misconception that travels across teams and deserves a direct answer: generated code that compiles has passed a syntax

What Compiling Does Not Prove — The related misconception is that a green test suite means review is unnecessary. Tests check what they test. A targeted
NARRATION

A misconception that travels across teams and deserves a direct answer: generated code that compiles has passed a syntax check. That is all. Compilation confirms that the code is grammatically valid in the language. It says nothing about whether the code does what was intended, whether it introduces a security vulnerability, whether it handles edge cases, or whether it regresses behavior that worked before. Code that compiles and fails is still a failure. The compiler is not a test suite.

// SLIDE 08 — THESIS

THE CORE CLAIM.

A misconception that travels across teams and deserves a direct answer: generated code that compiles has passed a syntax

The related misconception is that a green test suite means review is unnecessary. Tests check what they test. A targeted

NARRATION

A misconception that travels across teams and deserves a direct answer: generated code that compiles has passed a syntax check. That is all. Compilation confirms that the code is grammatically valid in the language. It says nothing about whether the code does what was intended, whether it introduces a security vulnerability, whether it handles edge cases, or whether it regresses behavior that worked before. Code that compiles and fails is still a failure. The compiler is not a test suite.
